unit.name: Model learning and model-based planning
unit.description: Shows how robots can learn forward dynamics models and then optimize actions through those models, with examples using pixel, keypoint, and particle-based state representations.
unit.summary: Motivated by the limits of model-free RL, Li proposes learning an approximate forward model of the world from robot interaction data and using it for planning [ts=2500s] [ts=2515s] [ts=2535s]. The planning problem is the inverse of prediction: given a current state and target state, the robot optimizes action sequences so the model’s predicted trajectory moves toward the target, usually executing only the first action and then replanning with fresh observations [ts=2561s] [ts=2574s] [ts=2617s]. He notes that GPUs make large-scale parallel sampling and optimization practical [ts=2633s] [ts=2641s]. A major design choice is state representation, illustrated through pixel dynamics (Deep Visual Foresight), keypoint dynamics for rigid pushing, and particle dynamics for granular media and dough manipulation [ts=2666s] [ts=2698s] [ts=2726s] [ts=2772s]. The particle-based examples show letter-shaping with granular materials and a dumpling-making system that chooses tools and actions while recovering from disturbances; in that dough domain, a learned dynamics model from real interactions beat a physics-based deformable simulator [ts=2856s] [ts=2891s] [ts=2950s] [ts=3248s] [ts=3270s].

key_points.text: Model learning trains a forward dynamics model from interaction data and uses it for downstream planning.
Planning solves the inverse problem: choose actions so the predicted future state approaches a target state.
A common loop is to optimize a trajectory, execute only the first action, observe the new state, and re-optimize.
GPU-enabled parallel sampling makes action-sequence optimization efficient enough to use learned models in practice.
Pixel dynamics predicts future images directly from actions.
Keypoint dynamics tracks 3D keypoints to plan rigid-object pushing.
Particle dynamics models granular media and dough, enabling tasks like letter rearrangement and dumpling making.
For deformable dough, the learned model from real interactions was more accurate than the physics-based MPM simulator.

kp_catalog.name: Learned forward dynamics for model-based planning
kp_catalog.description: Instead of directly learning a policy, a robot can learn how actions change the world and use that model to optimize future action sequences.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because learned forward dynamics for model-based planning formalizes how instead of directly learning a policy, a robot can learn how actions change the world and use that model to optimize future action sequences, which later units use as core machinery rather than background context.

kp_catalog.name: State representations for learned world models
kp_catalog.description: World models can represent state at different granularities—pixels, keypoints, or particles—and the right choice depends on the task and object structure.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because state representations for learned world models clarifies world models can represent state at different granularities—pixels, keypoints, or particles—and the right choice depends on the task and object structure, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.

kp_catalog.name: Model-free RL successes and sim-to-real limitations
kp_catalog.description: RL has produced dramatic wins in games and locomotion, yet still suffers from sample inefficiency, safety concerns, and limited robustness in many manipulation settings.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because model-free RL successes and sim-to-real limitations clarifies rL has produced dramatic wins in games and locomotion, yet still suffers from sample inefficiency, safety concerns, and limited robustness in many manipulation settings, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.
